context: they say in Bethlehem of Judea, for thus it is written by the prophet, they knew the prophecy, they knew Micah, that's what they're going to reference in verse number six, Micah five and verse two
point_summary: Micah 5:2 pinpoints Bethlehem as the prophesied birthplace of the Messiah, a prophecy the religious leaders knew but were not actively seeking to see fulfilled.

context: He says that we are not to think of him, this charge to every man, not to think of himself more highly than he ought to think. It reminds me of Micah 6 and verse number 8 where the Bible says, he hath showed thee, O man, what is good and what doth the Lord require of thee, but to do justly and to love mercy and to walk humbly with thy God.
point_summary: Micah 6:8's command to "walk humbly with thy God" is invoked as the scriptural counterpart to Paul's charge in Romans 12:3 not to think of oneself more highly than one ought.

context: the word of the Lord that came to Micah the Moresthi in the days of Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah, kings of Judah, which he saw concerning Samaria and Jerusalem.
point_summary: Micah 1:1 is treated as a chronological anchor, its list of three Judean kings (Jotham, Ahaz, Hezekiah) establishing the precise historical period in which Micah's prophecy was delivered.

context: The Bible says this, He hath showed thee, O man, what is good, and what doth the Lord require of thee, but to do justly, to love mercy, and to walk humbly with thy God.
point_summary: Micah 6:8 is introduced as the sermon's controlling text, with its threefold requirement—do justly, love mercy, walk humbly—framed by the question "what doth the Lord require of thee?" as the message's title and focus.

context: Micah 6, one through eight
point_summary: Micah 6:1–8 is introduced as a unit whose climactic question in verse 8 echoes Moses' identical covenant demand in Deuteronomy 10:12–13, linking prophet and Pentateuch on what God requires of his people.

context: Micah 6, one through eight
point_summary: Micah 6:8's question "What doth the Lord require of thee?" echoes Deuteronomy 10:12–13, establishing that God's covenant demands on Israel remained consistent across centuries of redemptive history.

context: We see it in Micah, right? We just read it there in verse number eight of chapter six. What doth the Lord require of thee but to fear the Lord thy God, to walk in all his ways, and to love him, and to serve the Lord thy God with all thy heart, with all thy soul, to keep the commandments of the Lord and his statutes which I commanded thee this day for thy good?
point_summary: Micah 6:8's question "What doth the Lord require of thee?" is shown to echo a consistent biblical demand—from Moses in Deuteronomy to Solomon in Ecclesiastes—of fearing God, loving Him, and keeping His commandments.

context: hear ye now what the Lord saith
point_summary: Micah 6:1 opens a divine courtroom scene in which God summons the mountains as witnesses to His controversy with Israel, demanding that His people testify against Him.

context: for the Lord hath a controversy
point_summary: God's covenant lawsuit against Israel, summoning creation itself as witness, frames the Lord's controversy with his people as the backdrop for what he truly requires of them.

context: ...wherewith shall I come before the Lord? And bow myself before the High God? Shall I come before Him with burnt offerings, with calves of a year old?
point_summary: Micah 6:6's question — "wherewith shall I come before the Lord?" — frames Israel's reply to God's indictment as a reliance on outward religious offerings while the heart remained far from Him.

context: He hath showed thee, O man, what is good. And what doth the Lord require of thee, but to do justly?
point_summary: Micah 6:8's call to "do justly" is grounded in God's revealed standard, not human self-definition, contrasting the judges-era pattern of every man doing right by his own authority.

context: The Bible tells us in Micah 3 and verse 11, the heads thereof judge, but notice what it says, they judge for reward. The priests thereof teach, but notice, they teach for hire. The prophets thereof divine, but notice, they divine for money. Yet, will they lean upon the Lord and say, is not the Lord among us? None evil can come upon us.
point_summary: Corrupt leaders in Micah's day perverted justice, teaching, and prophecy by making personal gain their motive rather than faithful service to God's standard.

context: The prince asketh, and the judge asketh for a reward. The great man, he uttereth his mischievous desire, so they wrap it up.
point_summary: Micah 7:3 illustrates the wholesale corruption of Israel's leadership—prince, judge, and great man alike—all pursuing personal reward rather than the just rule God requires.

context: It is God's instruction here in the passage in Micah 6 when it's the indictment, the controversy the Lord has with His people.
point_summary: Against a backdrop of corrupt leaders who judged, taught, and prophesied for personal gain, Micah 6 presents God's indictment of His people alongside His remedy: do justly and love mercy.

context: This is what is good. This is what's acceptable to God. This is what He requires of me. Number one, to do justly, to do right. Number two, to love mercy.
point_summary: Micah 6:8's call to "love mercy" moves beyond receiving God's mercy to actively showing it, grounded in the merciful kindness embodied in Christ and made acceptable to God.

context: I consider the message from Micah here in Micah 6 and verse 8 as he's ministering to these people that God's called him to minister to.
point_summary: Loving mercy, as Micah 6:8 demands, means not merely welcoming God's mercy as its recipient but actively showing it to others—a disposition inseparable from humble, submissive walking with God.

reference_text: He hath showed thee, O man, what is good, and what doth the Lord require of thee
context: The prophet says, the Lord hath a controversy with his people. Okay, what do we do? We bring some offerings? Do all these things? Is that going to make God happy? He says, He hath showed thee, O man, what is good, and what doth the Lord require of thee?
point_summary: Walking humbly with God, as Micah 6:8 requires, is illustrated by Christ's own humility in Philippians 2 and John 13, set against Israel's wickedness as the backdrop for what God truly demands.

context: To do justly, to love mercy, and to walk humbly with thy God.
point_summary: Against the backdrop of Ahaz's wickedness and Israel's misguided attempts at appeasement, Micah 6:8 is presented as God's clear, sufficient answer to what He requires: do justly, love mercy, and walk humbly with Him.

context: What doth the Lord require of thee? Micah says this to those that he's called to minister. Here's what God's shown us, three things, do justly, love mercy, walk humbly with thy God.
point_summary: Micah 6:8's three requirements—do justly, love mercy, walk humbly with God—are presented as God's own revealed standard for right living, urgently applicable amid contemporary cultural confusion.

context: And thou Bethlehem, this is the record of Micah five in verse number two in the land of Judah, art not the least among the princes of Judah for out of these shall come a governor that shall rule my people Israel.
point_summary: Micah 5:2's prophecy that the ruler of Israel would come out of Bethlehem is cited as the scriptural fulfillment explaining why God's providence brought Mary and Joseph to that specific town.

context: He will turn again, He will have compassion upon us, He will subdue our iniquities, and Thou wilt cast all their sins into the depths of the sea.
point_summary: God's casting of sins into the depths of the sea (Mic. 7:19) illustrates the totality of forgiveness secured through redemption, paired with Psalm 103:12 to show sin's complete removal.

context: That they're buried in the deepest sea.
point_summary: God's forgiveness of sin is pictured as casting transgressions into the depths of the sea, confirming that the believer's debt is fully and permanently removed.

context: the prophecy that Jesus, the Messiah, would be born in Bethlehem
point_summary: Micah 5:2 is noted only in passing as a familiar messianic text before the sermon pivots to Micah 5:6 and its reference to "the land of Nimrod"; Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: they shall waste the land of Assyria with the sword and the land of Nimrod in the entrances thereof
point_summary: Micah 5:6's reference to Assyria as "the land of Nimrod" demonstrates that Nimrod's legacy was so enduring that his name identified that entire region centuries later, from Genesis through the prophetic era.

context: In Micah 5 it prophesies of the place in which Jesus would be born.
point_summary: Old Testament prophecy specified not only that Messiah would be born but precisely where—Bethlehem, Ephrathah—a detail the scribes knew yet failed to act upon.

context: Who is a God like unto thee that pardoneth iniquity, that passeth by the transgression of the remnant of his heritage? He retaineth not his anger forever because...
point_summary: God's delight in mercy, not wrath, is confirmed in Micah 7:18, which declares that pardoning iniquity flows from his pleasure in mercy rather than any delight in judgment.

context: just as it does here in Micah 7, 18, that he delighteth in mercy
point_summary: God's delight in mercy—not in wrath or judgment—is presented as a consistent biblical theme, with Micah 7:18 offered as a defining declaration that it is God's joy to show mercy to fallen sinners.

context: He will turn again, He will have compassion upon us, He will subdue our iniquities, and that will cast all their sins into the depths of the sea
point_summary: God's forgiveness is so complete that, as Micah 7:19 declares, He casts all sins into the depths of the sea—a picture of total, irreversible pardon secured through Christ's atoning payment.

context: where is he that is born King of the Jews, the going ghetto, and Bethlehem of Judah, for thus it is written in the prophets. And they reference back to Micah chapter 5 in verse number 2
point_summary: Micah 5:2 is cited as the prophetic basis by which the chief priests and scribes identified Bethlehem as the birthplace of the Messiah when the magi inquired.

context: Micah chapter number five, the place where Christ would be born
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: but thou Bethlehem Ephrata, though thou be little among the thousands of Judah, yet out of thee shall he come forth unto me that is to be ruler in Israel, whose goings forth have been from old, from everlasting
point_summary: Micah 5:2's prophecy of Bethlehem as the birthplace of Israel's eternal ruler was so precisely known that Israel's own religious experts could immediately identify it when Herod demanded to know where Christ would be born.

context: they quote from Micah five, and thou Bethlehem in the land of Judah, art not the least among the princes of Judah, for out of thee shall come a governor that shall rule my people Israel
point_summary: Micah 5:2's precise prediction of Bethlehem as Messiah's birthplace was known by Israel's own religious experts, yet their familiarity with the prophecy did not move them to seek the Christ.
